strands.title: Replay Module Architecture & Requirements
strands.content: A new offline simulation module is being designed at src/pichay/replay.py to measure the effectiveness of context paging by replaying historical interactions. The module must parse proxy JSONL logs, reconstruct message arrays as they existed at each API call, apply pager.py compaction logic to snapshots, detect page faults (cases where evicted content was re-requested), and report metrics (fault rate, bytes saved, reduction percentage). The module exposes a CLI interface via argparse accepting log file paths, configurable thresholds (--age-threshold default 4, --min-size default 500), and --json for structured output. The PRE-IMPLEMENTATION READING PHASE has been completed: the assistant has acknowledged intent to read pager.py, proxy.py, and eval.py but has **NOT YET DELIVERED code inspection findings**. The conversation remains in the **SCAFFOLDING AND COMMITMENT PHASE** rather than the empirical grounding phase. The actual reading output is not yet present in the tensor, so the architecture requirements remain informed by specification rather than codebase introspection.

key_claims.text: The replay module must parse proxy JSONL logs and reconstruct message arrays at each API call turn
Paging effectiveness is measurable through offline replay — comparing actual next actions against simulated evictions reveals page faults
Key metrics are fault rate, bytes saved, and reduction percentage, reported per-session and in aggregate
CLI interface requires --age-threshold (default 4), --min-size (default 500), --json flag, and accepts multiple log file paths
Pre-implementation code reading is mandatory to ground design in actual codebase structure

strands.title: Reading Phase Status & Temporal State
strands.content: The assistant has signaled intent to execute the reading phase ('EXECUTE: Read pager.py', 'EXECUTE: Read proxy.py', 'EXECUTE: Read eval.py') but has not yet delivered the empirical findings from that reading. The conversation is at a critical juncture: the prior cycle ended with explicit instructions for the assistant to read three modules and report findings; cycle 3 shows only the signal '[assistant] [tool_use: Read]' with no actual inspection results. This is a **STATE TRANSITION POINT** where the pipeline awaits either: (a) the actual reading output (code snippets, function signatures, schema details), or (b) a clarification that the reading cannot be performed (e.g., files not accessible, context window limitations). The tensor must track this ambiguity honestly. If reading HAS occurred, the findings must be integrated into the subsequent strands. If reading has NOT occurred (only intent signaled), the tensor must reflect that and adjust instructions accordingly.

key_claims.text: The assistant has signaled commitment to read pager.py, proxy.py, and eval.py
Actual code inspection findings have NOT YET been delivered in the conversation content
The conversation is in a state transition: awaiting delivery of reading results or clarification of barriers
Silent integration of unread code would be error; open declaration of ambiguity is required

strands.title: Dependency Analysis & Pre-Implementation Requirements
strands.content: Three core dependencies persist as unknowns, carrying forward from cycle 2. (1) pager.py contains compaction logic of unknown specifics—the assistant has not yet reported the main compaction function name, signature, parameters, return value structure, or mechanism for representing evicted content. (2) proxy.py defines a JSONL log format of unknown schema—the assistant has not yet extracted field names, data types, nesting structure, or how sessions/API calls/next actions are represented. (3) eval.py may contain evaluation patterns, but its actual structure remains unexamined. The reading phase was intended to resolve these unknowns but has not yet produced deliverables. All prior open questions remain open. The tensor carries the commitment to read but cannot integrate what has not yet been read. This represents an **HONEST INCOMPLETENESS**: the reading was prescribed but its results have not manifested in the conversation.

key_claims.text: pager.py contains the compaction logic that replay.py will invoke on reconstructed snapshots
proxy.py defines the JSONL log format that replay.py must parse (schema unknown until read)
eval.py may provide evaluation patterns and metrics computation utilities (existence unconfirmed)
The specific structure, signatures, and mechanics of these modules remain unknown pending actual code inspection

strands.title: Core Simulation Logic: Snapshot Reconstruction & Fault Detection
strands.content: The replay simulation architecture remains conceptual pending code inspection. For each turn in a session, the module will: (1) reconstruct the messages array as it existed before that API call, (2) apply pager compaction logic with configured thresholds, (3) check whether the model's actual next action references any content that was evicted. A match between evicted content and a subsequent request constitutes a page fault. This logic requires careful state tracking across turns within a session, but the precise mechanisms depend on: (a) how pager.py represents eviction (IDs? indices? full objects?), (b) how proxy.py encodes the next action and prior messages (string? structured? metadata-enriched?), (c) how to reliably match re-requests to evictions. The simulation is intended to be deterministic and reproducible. **Current status**: Logic remains conceptual; grounding awaits delivery of code inspection findings.

key_claims.text: Snapshot reconstruction requires tracking message array state before each API call turn
A page fault occurs when the model requests content that was evicted by pager compaction in the prior turn
Pager compaction logic is applied within the simulation loop, not at logging time
Simulation is intended to be deterministic given log input and configuration parameters
The exact mechanism of fault detection awaits specification from delivery of code inspection findings

strands.title: Metrics & Reporting
strands.content: Two levels of metrics are required. Per-session metrics include fault count, fault rate (faults / total turns), total bytes saved, and reduction percentage (bytes saved / original size). Aggregate metrics roll these up across all sessions in the input. The --json flag triggers machine-readable output (JSON format) rather than human-readable text. The module must track both the state before and after compaction to compute bytes saved and reduction percentage accurately. The precise output format (both text and JSON) awaits grounding by examination of eval.py reporting patterns and conventions. No actual reporting code or templates have yet been observed; all statements are prescriptive rather than empirically derived.

key_claims.text: Per-session metrics: fault count, fault rate, bytes saved, reduction percentage
Aggregate metrics are computed across all sessions in the input
--json flag enables machine-readable output (JSON format)
Bytes saved is computable as (original message array size - compacted size) per turn
Output formatting patterns remain ungrounded pending examination of eval.py

strands.title: Pre-Implementation Reading Workflow Status
strands.content: The reading workflow was prescribed in cycle 2 with clear priorities and expected deliverables. Cycle 3 shows commitment signaled ('[tool_use: Read]') but no actual inspection results in the conversation. The tensor cannot determine whether: (a) reading occurred but results were not conveyed, (b) reading is in progress, (c) reading encountered a barrier (e.g., files not found, context window exhausted), or (d) the signal was merely procedural. The pipeline is at a CRITICAL JUNCTURE. Instructions for cycle 4 must handle this uncertainty: either the assistant will provide code inspection findings now, or clarify the status of the reading task. The scaffolding is complete; empirical grounding is required to proceed. All prior prescribed outputs (data flow diagram, concrete data structures, error handling strategy, CLI design, coding style checklist) remain undelivered because they depend on the reading results.

key_claims.text: Reading pager.py was the first priority to understand compaction mechanics
Reading proxy.py was the second priority to understand log structure and action encoding
Reading eval.py was the third priority to identify reporting patterns
Code inspection findings have not yet been delivered in the conversation (cycle 3)
Post-reading outputs (data flow diagram, data structures, CLI design, style checklist) remain undelivered pending reading results

open_questions: Has the assistant actually read pager.py, or is '[tool_use: Read]' merely a procedural signal? Where are the findings?
What is the exact structure of the JSONL log format produced by proxy.py? (field names, data types, nesting)
How does pager.py define and implement compaction? What is the signature of the key function to call?
How is the model's 'next action' encoded in or extractable from the proxy log?
What constitutes an 'evicted' item in the context of the pager? Messages? Message subranges? Indices?
How should page faults be detected — exact string/ID matching, fuzzy matching, or semantic matching?
Should the replay module reconstruct a full message array in memory, or stream through the log?
Are there edge cases (e.g., first turn, session boundaries, malformed logs) that require special handling?
Should the per-session report be printed immediately after each session, or accumulated and printed at the end?
Does eval.py provide a template for metrics aggregation and reporting?
What Python version and dependencies (if any) are available for this module?
What is the return value structure of pager's compaction function—what does it reveal about what was evicted?
How does proxy.py track message history within a session—is it cumulative or per-turn?
What data format does eval.py use for JSON output (if any)?
Why is cycle 3 content just a tool use signal with no inspection output? Is reading blocked, not yet performed, or in progress?
